Years of Upheavals : India in early 20th century

# Years of Upheavals : India in early 20th century

### by NCRGOJO

[](https://substack.com/@cshc)

![CSHC's avatar](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!Y9T4!,w_36,h_36,c_fill,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2Fc61a8328-b4cb-4e00-88aa-b73e4963a670_225x225.jpeg)

[CSHC](https://substack.com/@cshc)

Jan 04, 2025

[](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!h3Qi!,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2Ffc80218a-f3ee-4e00-858e-0cd77db7fbfe_700x536.jpeg)

![](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!h3Qi!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2Ffc80218a-f3ee-4e00-858e-0cd77db7fbfe_700x536.jpeg)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

British rule in India, which began in 1757 after the battle of Plassey and became an empire-building project in the subsequent decades, perhaps was the most transformative period for the Indian subcontinent and its inhabitants. For some, it is the period that saw the rise of industrialisation in India, the destruction of the redundant and oppressive feudal class, and for some, it is the establishment of an extractive, bloodsucking administration that drained India of its wealth and left it in abject poverty and destitution. Casting these debates aside, we shall examine the political developments that shaped our history and will try to see how the decisions and developments changed the course of our country. After the revolt and the Government of India Act of 1858, which brought India under the direct control of the British crown, several measures were taken by the colonial administration to incorporate India into its empire fold. Indian councils and the formation of municipalities were steps in establishing a colonial administration in India. Limited self-government and some representation were gradually provided to Indians through the India Council Act of 1909 and the Government of India Act of 1919; the goal was quite precisely highlighted: to deny the complete independence of India, keeping her as a dominion in the Empire. More election reforms, though of limited character, were introduced over a period of time, with the Government of India Act 1935 being the final one before independence. We shall survey the period that saw several developments, turmoil, and agitations that finally culminated in the final scheme of partition and independence of India. This article will largely focus on the first half of the period, namely pre-1930s, and will highlight the groundwork laid by all the actors.

### **Early 20th C & The Change of Hearts**

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

The new century began with a new hope and building up of aspirations in the mind of urban elite Indians. Over a period of time, their dissent and dissatisfaction against the British administration had grown, but still there was an element of “affection” for the Raj, and almost all the dissenters used to profess loyalty to the Empire. Up until then, this new class of Indians, which grew out of the transformation in the society and gradual educational reforms introduced by the Raj, saw this relationship as paternal. All of these feelings finally vaporised when Lord Curzon announced the scheme for the partition of Bengal. Lord Curzon’s anti-Indian stance, with severe displays of Hinduphobia and several unpopular measures like the University Act of 1904, the Indian Official Secrets Amendment Act of 1904, and the Calcutta Municipal Amendment Act of 1899, has already created a roar among the nationalist populace. But the Partition of Bengal was seen as a great betrayal, and the entire Bengal erupted. For the Muslim elites in Bengal, however, this was seen as an opportunity. This was one of the first instances of the British administration taking advantage of the Hindu-Muslim fault line. Curzon highlighted the hidden objective of the British government in his Dacca speech in 1904, where he underscored that Muslim interests would be more protected in a separate province. Hence, as noted by Sekhar Bandopadhyay, elite Muslims led by the nawab of Dacca rallied round this scheme. Hindus of Bengal launched one of the first mass movements of the Indian independence struggle, the Swadeshi movement, and for the first time passive struggle measures were deployed on such a mass scale against the government. Leaders called for a boycott of British goods and universities and resignation from government jobs. Nationalist Indians set up Swadeshi enterprises and opened colleges as an alternative to the British resources. The movement turned more violent, and a few leaders (the extremists) called for widening the movement. The Indian National Congress, which was already undergoing an ideological strife, finally went through a catastrophic showdown in the Surat session (1907) where the meeting turned into a fight and got split. Over the few years, the movement slowly lost its momentum due to government repression and the retirement of many leaders like Pal and Aurobindo Ghosh. The episode of the partition of Bengal created ruffles in Indian society, where it inspired many future leaders, but it also was the reason for the increase in dissatisfaction among the youth, who, in response to this hopeless situation, took more extreme measures. One more result of the Swadeshi movement was the formation of the Muslim League and the British establishment’s change of attitude towards the Muslims in India. Muslims were particularly disappointed with the announcement of the annulment of the partition in 1911, though the expressions of loyal support for the Raj remained. We shall see how this relationship developed in the subsequent sections.

### **Idea of Pakistan and the Muslim League**

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

The timeline of the idea of Pakistan is a much more controversial and debatable subject that has the ability to charge the emotions of every person, irrespective of what side of the spectrum he/she lies. Much has been written about it, spoken about it, and lied about it. Every side of the political spectrum accuses their opponents of being responsible for this tragedy.

The two-nation theory on which Pakistan as an idea was developed was first proposed by Sir Syed Ahmed Khan, pioneer of the Aligarh movement, who highlighted the distinction between two communities and emphasised that India is not a nation of common shared culture but an amalgamation of various *quams* or communities of a common descent. He openly advocated for creating a dissenting front against Congress, who he saw as an organisation of Hindus conspiring against the Muslims. His advocacy for loyalism towards the British also greatly helped the elite Muslims in particular, who were the most affected group due to the failure of the revolt of 1857. The British crackdown after the revolt greatly affected this group, who enjoyed the ruling power under the patronage of Mughal emperors.

In the late 19th century, a faction in the Aligarh movement too began the criticism of the imperial rule, and many younger leaders felt that British administration was more sympathetic to the Hindu populace. The Hindu-Urdu controversy only aggravated their frustrations. The final straw came with the rumours of the introduction of the representative government, which alarmed the Muslim leaders, who felt that the Muslim community would become completely powerless in front of the brutal Hindu majority. Hence, a group of leaders led by Aga Khan in 1906 met the viceroy in Shimla, and subsequently, the Muslim League was formed, whose goals were to safeguard Muslim community interests and preach loyalty to the British. The meeting was a huge success for the elite Muslims as their demand for special representation was accepted and the concept of separate electorates was introduced in the Indian Council Act of 1909. A few more concessions were provided for the Muslims; for instance, income qualification was lower for the Muslims. Therefore, this strategic partnership between Muslim elites and the British government was firmly established. For the British it was a splendid opportunity to create a firm opposition to the Congress menace and finally checkmate the growing Indian national movement, but this relationship was in for a rocky start. A new leadership was emerging in the Muslim elites who were not particularly interested in toeing the path of professing loyalty laid out by Sir Syed Ahmed Khan. Leaders of this faction included the Ali brothers and M.A. Jinnah. The annulment of the partition of Bengal in 1911 and the setback in the Muslim University campaign strengthened their position, and the idea of British patronage suffered a dent. But it was the outbreak of World War I that brought this dissatisfaction to its focal point.

### Outbreak of War and Khilafat Movement

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

World War One began with the assassination of Archduke Franz Ferdinand of Austria, and soon all of Europe was engulfed in the war. Things took an interesting turn when the Ottoman Empire declared support for Germany and entered the war. Isthiaq Ahmed notes the pro-Ottoman sentiments among the Sunni majority for whom the sultan was seen as the caliph. Ottoman Turkey was also the only independent Muslim power. Though the British administration was very concerned about the loyalty of Indian Muslims, who contributed a significant number to the army and were deployed in the major theatres of war, Indian Muslims largely remained loyal to the Raj with few incidents. Meanwhile, in order to curb any pro-Turkey sentiments in India, the government arrested the Ali brothers ( Mohmmad Ali & Shukar Ali, who later became the biggest proponents of the Khilafat movement). This added fuel to the growing discomfort of the elite Muslims regarding the attitude of the British towards Turkey in the war, which opened a great opportunity, as was interpreted by many Congress leaders, to bridge the gap between the Muslims and Hindus finally and forever and create a united front against the imperial yoke. Jinnah was a member of both the Congress and the Muslim League and acted as the mediator, and the famous Lucknow Pact of 1916 was signed. Sarojini Naidu called Jinnah the ambassador of Hindu-Muslim unity for this achievement. One of the key concessions of this pact was the demand of separate electorates was accepted by the Congress.Nevertheless, the pact was hailed a success, and for the first time, the Muslim League and Congress were ready to cooperate and set aside their differences. In the meantime, Turkey was defeated, which enlarged the narrative of ‘Islam in danger,’ which led to the launching of the Khilafat movement, whose objective, as Ishtiaq Ahmed mentions, was the “retention of the Caliphate and its control over Muslim holy lands.”

The Khilafat committee knew that the movement required the support of the Hindus and saw Mahatma Gandhi as the leader with the capability to help them. Gandhi too didn't want to miss this opportunity to bring the Muslims into the national movement fold. After the arrival of Mahatma Gandhi from South Africa in 1915, he led a successful satyagraha in Champaran, Kheda, and Ahmedabad, where he was able to rally the masses who up until then were untouched by independence struggle consciousness. Meanwhile, the historical declaration by then Secretary of State for India Lord Montagu in 1917 at the House of Commons in which he announced an objective of “gradual development of self-governing institutions, with a view to the progressive realisation of responsible in India as an integral part of the British Empire” (From Plassey to Partition, Sekhar Bandopadhyay). The Government of India Act of 1919 was implemented, and the concept of dyarchy was introduced. The act didn't offer self-government or dominion status and failed to satisfy the demands of politically active Indians. In 1919, British administration implemented the Rowlatt Act, perhaps the most draconian act in the history of British administration in India, which gave powers to police to arrest any person without any reason and introduced more control over the press. Mahatma Gandhi launched a satyagraha against the act. Lala Lajpat Rai was killed in a brutal lathi charge while protesting against the act. Gandhi launched the satyagraha against the Rowlatt Act in March 1919 but didn't find any support from the Congress. On April 13th, 1919, General Dyer opened fire on unarmed civilians who gathered in Jallianwala Bagh for the Baisakhi festival, and the country erupted in violence, which led to Gandhi calling it off.

The Muslim League was very concerned with the recent developments that had “evoked strong reactions from the Muslim elites” (Ishtiaq Ahmed in his book "Jinnah"). With the growing uncertainty regarding the fate of the Caliph, the Muslim League was divided into three camps. The majority of the members were in favour of the non-cooperation struggle similar to Gandhi's agenda against the British; one camp wanted to remain loyal to the British government, and the third camp, which included Jinnah, was opposed to the non-cooperation movement but at the same time didn't want to cooperate with the government in the working of the 1919 reforms.In May 1920, the Treaty of Sevres with Turkey and the Hunter Commission report were published. Both of them brewed a storm in the Indian political circles. Though encountering stiff opposition, Gandhi was able to take Congress in confidence, and the non-cooperation program resolution was passed at the special Calcutta session. Jinnah, though agreeing with the premise of non-cooperation, was against a total all-out agitation against the government. He was irritated by Gandhi's passive resistance program, and the final breaking point came in the Nagpur session, where he was heckled during his speech when he addressed Mahatma Gandhi as “Mr. Gandhi” & Maulana Mohammad Ali “ Mr. Mohmmad Ali. He walked out of the session and left the Congress forever.

The non-cooperation movement was launched in 1921, and the first mass movement, which had the support of the two communities, began. Boycotts of foreign goods, encouragement of national schools, and arbitration courts were endorsed. He preferred the constitutional methods over passive resistance. Gandhi appealed for strong middle-class participation, particularly from students. Peasants and the working class had significantly participated in the movement. Gradually the movement turned more militant, and several incidents of riots were reported, the most horrific being the Chauri Chaura incident. The Khilafat movement too had turned more militant and was increasingly taking on more of an Islamic character. Usage of religious slogans and hardline Islamic rhetoric. Leaders like Savarkar have already raised the concerns of Gandhi, amplifying extraterritorial loyalties among the Indian Muslims. The Moplah massacre in Kerala and some other incidents of riots and attacks against the Hindus have increasingly made it clear that for the Khilafat leaders, the idea of non-violence is a matter of convenience. Therefore, in order to control the masses and avoid more bloodshed, Gandhi called off the movement. The outbreak of communal riots in 1922-23, which rocked the country and particularly affected North India, has demonstrated to every politically conscious Indian that the Hindu-Muslim alliance that Gandhi forged on thin lines has broken down. The nationalist revolution in Turkey abolished the Khilafat in 1924, and gradually the Khilafat movement too died out. Gandhi's grand dream of establishing a united front against the imperial administration suffered a major setback. The country was divided more than ever, and the gloomy clouds were hovering over the future of the Independence struggle.
